ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef tataki; 'beef food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the closest broad ancestor. A more specific tataki preparation term is absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated tataki code. A0CKW (Beef, lightly cooked) is the closest defensible parent given the minimal searing preparation method used in tataki. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Japanese preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for beef tataki as a specific dish preparation.

preparation_method: topped with pickled daikon and crispy shallots

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of beef tataki is approximately 120g, presented as thin slices of lightly seared angus flat iron beef arranged on a plate with wasabi dressing, pickled daikon, and crispy shallots.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Tataki — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Tataki (cr_id G00010988), which provides per-100g values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef flat iron steak, lightly cooked (FDC ID 174033), and set to -1 where not available in either source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate beef preparation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef tataki. GI is estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the analogous category of high-protein, low-carbohydrate beef dishes with minimal added sugars. The carbohydrate content of 4.19g/100g is primarily from the wasabi dressing and pickled daikon components. Glycemic load per 120g serving = (30 × 4.19 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 1.5, classified as very low. Source: University of Sydney GI Database framework and Atkinson et al. (2021) AJCN international GI tables.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 369mg/100g is in the borderline range (200–400mg); however, potassium at 368mg/100g provides a favourable counterbalance with a near 1:1 potassium-to-sodium ratio.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request wasabi dressing and any soy-based dipping sauces on the side, as these condiments can substantially increase the sodium load beyond the 369mg/100g baseline; total meal sodium should be monitored carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (4.19g/100g) and high protein (18.48g/100g) support stable postprandial blood glucose; estimated GI of approximately 30 places this dish in the low-GI category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef tataki is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces; pairing with low-GI vegetables rather than white rice further supports glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.3g/100g and cholesterol at 52mg/100g are moderate; sodium at 369mg/100g warrants attention for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 100–120g, avoid additional soy-based sauces to control sodium intake, and note that the lean flat iron cut provides a relatively favourable saturated fat profile compared to higher-fat beef preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokines.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with seared salmon or tuna tataki to obtain omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit; if beef tataki is consumed, request reduced-sodium ponzu and retain ginger components for their gingerol anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Tataki (牛のたたき, Gyū no Tataki) is a Japanese preparation of thinly sliced beef — in this Singaporean restaurant version using angus flat iron — that is lightly seared on the outside while remaining raw or very rare at the centre. The technique originates from the Japanese tataki method, which involves briefly searing the surface of meat or fish over high heat to develop a flavourful crust while preserving the tender, raw interior. This dish is served with a wasabi dressing and topped with pickled daikon radish and crispy fried shallots, adding sharp, tangy, and crunchy contrasts to the silky, umami-rich beef. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 148 kcal, 18.48g protein, 6.0g fat, and only 4.19g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 30. Sodium is 369mg/100g, which is borderline for hypertension management, though the near-equal potassium content (368mg/100g) provides a favourable counterbalance. The dish is suitable for type 2 diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and high protein profile. It is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat. Commonly served as a starter or light main course at Japanese restaurants in Singapore, it is popular at lunch and dinner occasions.
common_user_queries: Is beef tataki raw or cooked?
Is beef tataki suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Tataki (cr_id G00010988) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for lightly cooked beef flat iron steak as the closest approved-source analogue. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database framework for high-protein, low-carbohydrate beef preparations.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.3g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.6g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.1g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef flat iron steak, lightly cooked (FDC ID 174033), as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown for this dish. These values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific beef tataki preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this exact dish formulation as of 2026. 3. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for beef tataki exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The low GI is defensible given the high protein and low carbohydrate content. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; traditional Japanese preparations may use mirin (contains alcohol) and soy sauce that may not be halal-certified. 5. The input health_suitability entries for diabetes and hypertension incorrectly set suitable: false despite the clinical reasoning supporting suitability; these have been corrected in health_context with appropriate concern levels. 6. FoodEx2 match type set to 'closest_parent' as the allowed values are 'closest_parent', 'exact', and 'none'; 'analogous_estimate' is not a valid FoodEx2 match type value and has been corrected accordingly.
